nodes.label: Roman Empire
nodes.summary: The post-Republican Roman state (27 BC – AD 476 in the west).

nodes.label: Hadrian's Wall
nodes.summary: The Roman Empire's northern frontier wall in Britain.

nodes.label: Mithraism
nodes.summary: A Roman mystery cult centred on the god Mithras (1st–4th c. AD).

nodes.label: Mithra
nodes.summary: An ancient Indo-Iranian deity of covenant and light.

nodes.label: Silk Road
nodes.summary: The network of trade routes linking the Mediterranean world to China.

nodes.label: Great Wall of China
nodes.summary: Fortifications built across China's northern frontier.

nodes.label: Euclid
nodes.summary: Greek mathematician, 'father of geometry', author of the Elements (c. 300 BC).

nodes.label: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi
nodes.summary: Persian polymath whose Arabic recension of Euclid's Elements was widely transmitted.

nodes.label: Jagannatha Samrat
nodes.summary: Indian astronomer at Jai Singh II's court who rendered Euclid's Elements into Sanskrit as the Rekhaganita.

nodes.label: Jai Singh II
nodes.summary: Rajput ruler of Amber/Jaipur and astronomer who founded the Jantar Mantar observatories.

nodes.label: Alexander the Great
nodes.summary: Macedonian king who conquered the Achaemenid Empire and campaigned as far as the Indus (356–323 BC).

nodes.label: Alexandria
nodes.summary: Egyptian city founded by Alexander the Great; seat of the Museum and Library where Euclid taught.

nodes.label: Buddhism
nodes.summary: A major religion founded in ancient India (5th c. BC), later carried along the Silk Road into East Asia.

nodes.label: Ancient Greece
nodes.summary: The Greek civilization whose art, philosophy and science shaped the Mediterranean world.

nodes.label: Athens
nodes.summary: The leading city-state of Ancient Greece; home of Plato's Academy and Aristotle's Lyceum.

nodes.label: Aristotle
nodes.summary: Greek philosopher and polymath, pupil of Plato and tutor of Alexander the Great (384–322 BC).

nodes.label: Plato
nodes.summary: Greek philosopher, founder of the Academy in Athens (c. 428–348 BC).

nodes.label: Homer
nodes.summary: The archaic Greek poet to whom the Iliad and Odyssey are attributed.

nodes.label: Greek mythology
nodes.summary: The body of myths of ancient Greece, later absorbed into Roman religion.

nodes.label: Ancient Egypt
nodes.summary: The civilization of the lower Nile, from its unification (c. 3100 BC) to Roman annexation (30 BC).

nodes.label: Cleopatra
nodes.summary: The last active pharaoh of Ptolemaic Egypt, who ruled from Alexandria (69–30 BC).

nodes.label: Nile
nodes.summary: The great river of northeastern Africa around which Ancient Egypt grew.

nodes.label: Great Pyramid of Giza
nodes.summary: The largest Egyptian pyramid, built as a tomb for the pharaoh Khufu (c. 2560 BC).

nodes.label: Egyptian hieroglyphs
nodes.summary: The formal writing system of Ancient Egypt.

nodes.label: al-Khwarizmi
nodes.summary: Persian mathematician at Baghdad's House of Wisdom, the 'father of algebra' (c. 780–850).

nodes.label: House of Wisdom
nodes.summary: The Abbasid library and translation institute in Baghdad that transmitted Greek learning into Arabic.

nodes.label: Baghdad
nodes.summary: The Abbasid capital and a Silk Road hub, founded in 762.

nodes.label: Abbasid Caliphate
nodes.summary: The third Islamic caliphate, seat of the Golden Age (750–1258).

nodes.label: Algebra
nodes.summary: The branch of mathematics whose name and discipline derive from al-Khwarizmi's Kitab al-Jabr.

nodes.label: Ptolemy
nodes.summary: Greco-Roman astronomer of Alexandria whose Almagest codified the geocentric model (c. 100–170 AD).

nodes.label: Nicolaus Copernicus
nodes.summary: Polish astronomer who placed the Sun at the centre of the cosmos (1473–1543).

nodes.label: Galileo Galilei
nodes.summary: Italian polymath whose telescopic observations defended Copernican heliocentrism (1564–1642).

nodes.label: Isaac Newton
nodes.summary: English mathematician and physicist, author of the Principia (1642–1727).

nodes.label: Confucius
nodes.summary: Chinese philosopher and teacher of the Spring and Autumn period whose ideas became Confucianism (551–479 BC).

nodes.label: Confucianism
nodes.summary: The ethical and philosophical tradition derived from the teachings of Confucius, central to Chinese and East Asian thought.

nodes.label: Tang dynasty
nodes.summary: A cosmopolitan Chinese dynasty (618–907), a golden age of Silk Road trade, poetry, and Buddhist and Confucian culture.

nodes.label: Japan
nodes.summary: The island nation of East Asia; its early Yamato state (from c. 3rd c. AD) adopted Chinese script, Buddhism, and Tang institutions.

nodes.label: Zen
nodes.summary: A meditation school of Mahayana Buddhism, descended from Chinese Chan, that became the pre-eminent Buddhist tradition of Japan.

nodes.label: Norse mythology
nodes.summary: The body of myths of the North Germanic peoples, centred on gods such as Odin and Thor.

nodes.label: Odin
nodes.summary: The chief god of Norse mythology, associated with wisdom, war, and death.

nodes.label: Thor
nodes.summary: The hammer-wielding Norse god of thunder, son of Odin.

nodes.label: Loki
nodes.summary: The shape-shifting trickster god of Norse mythology.

nodes.label: Celtic mythology
nodes.summary: The mythology of the Iron Age Celtic peoples of western Europe.

nodes.label: Paper
nodes.summary: A thin material for writing and printing, whose manufacture was perfected in Han China and spread west along the Silk Road.

nodes.label: Cai Lun
nodes.summary: The Han-dynasty court official traditionally credited with standardizing papermaking (c. 105 AD).

nodes.label: Woodblock printing
nodes.summary: An early printing technique developed in Tang China, spread by demand for Buddhist scriptures.

nodes.label: Gunpowder
nodes.summary: An explosive discovered by Tang-dynasty Chinese alchemists, one of the Four Great Inventions.

nodes.label: Compass
nodes.summary: A direction-finding instrument whose earliest form, a lodestone 'south-pointer', originated in Han China.

nodes.label: Mali Empire
nodes.summary: A West African empire (c. 1235–1670) enriched by the trans-Saharan gold trade and famed under Mansa Musa.

nodes.label: Mansa Musa
nodes.summary: The tenth mansa (emperor) of Mali, whose 1324 hajj to Mecca displayed the empire's legendary wealth.

nodes.label: Timbuktu
nodes.summary: A city on the southern edge of the Sahara that became a celebrated centre of trans-Saharan trade and Islamic learning.

nodes.label: Trans-Saharan trade
nodes.summary: The caravan trade in gold, salt, and ideas across the Sahara linking West Africa to the Mediterranean and Islamic worlds.

nodes.label: Islam
nodes.summary: A monotheistic Abrahamic religion founded in 7th-century Arabia, whose caliphates carried it across Africa and Asia.

nodes.label: Elizabeth II
nodes.summary: Queen of the United Kingdom from 1952 to 2022, whose descent runs back through the medieval English kings.

nodes.label: Alfred the Great
nodes.summary: King of Wessex who resisted the Viking invasions; ancestor of the later English monarchs.

nodes.label: House of Wessex
nodes.summary: The Anglo-Saxon royal house of Wessex, whose recorded genealogies traced its line back to the god Woden.

nodes.label: Naruhito
nodes.summary: The reigning Emperor of Japan (from 2019), 126th of a line traditionally traced to the legendary Jimmu.

nodes.label: Jimmu
nodes.summary: The legendary first emperor of Japan, held to be a descendant of the sun goddess Amaterasu.

nodes.label: Amaterasu
nodes.summary: The sun goddess of Shinto, from whom the Japanese imperial line traditionally claims descent.

nodes.label: Shinto
nodes.summary: The indigenous religion of Japan, centred on kami such as the sun goddess Amaterasu.

nodes.label: Renaissance
nodes.summary: The European revival of classical learning and art that began in 14th-century Florence.

nodes.label: Renaissance humanism
nodes.summary: The Renaissance revival of study of the Greek and Roman classics, centred on Florence.

nodes.label: Florence
nodes.summary: Tuscan city founded as a Roman colony, and the birthplace of the Renaissance.

nodes.label: House of Medici
nodes.summary: The Florentine banking dynasty whose patronage funded much of the Renaissance.

nodes.label: Leonardo da Vinci
nodes.summary: Florentine-trained painter, engineer and anatomist of the High Renaissance (1452-1519).

nodes.label: Michelangelo
nodes.summary: Florentine sculptor and painter of the High Renaissance (1475-1564).

nodes.label: Mona Lisa
nodes.summary: Leonardo's portrait, painted in Florence and now the best-known painting in the world.

nodes.label: Printing press
nodes.summary: Gutenberg's movable-type press (c. 1440), which made books cheap enough to spread ideas at scale.

nodes.label: Johannes Gutenberg
nodes.summary: Mainz goldsmith who introduced movable-type printing to Europe (c. 1400-1468).

nodes.label: Fall of Constantinople
nodes.summary: The Ottoman capture of Constantinople in 1453, which ended the Byzantine Empire.

nodes.label: Hinduism
nodes.summary: The major religion of the Indian subcontinent, its earliest layer in the Vedic period (from c. 1500 BC).

nodes.label: Sanskrit
nodes.summary: The classical language of the Indian subcontinent, an Indo-Aryan tongue descended from Proto-Indo-European and the liturgical language of Hinduism and Buddhism.

nodes.label: Maurya Empire
nodes.summary: The first empire to unify most of the Indian subcontinent (c. 322-185 BC), founded by Chandragupta Maurya.

nodes.label: Ashoka
nodes.summary: Third Mauryan emperor (c. 304-232 BC) who, after the Kalinga War, embraced Buddhism and spread it across Asia.

nodes.label: Chola dynasty
nodes.summary: A Tamil dynasty of southern India (9th-13th c. AD) that became a dominant Indian Ocean naval and trading power.

nodes.label: Srivijaya
nodes.summary: A maritime empire based in Sumatra (7th-13th c. AD) controlling the Strait of Malacca and a major centre of Buddhist learning.

nodes.label: Khmer Empire
nodes.summary: A Southeast Asian empire (802-1431 AD) centred on Angkor in present-day Cambodia, Hindu then Buddhist in its state religion.

nodes.label: Angkor Wat
nodes.summary: A temple complex in Cambodia built in the early 12th century as the state temple of the Khmer Empire, dedicated to Vishnu.

nodes.label: Borobudur
nodes.summary: A 9th-century Mahayana Buddhist monument in central Java, the largest Buddhist temple in the world.

statements.subject: mithraism
statements.evidence: Mithraism was a mystery cult practised across the Roman Empire (1st–4th c. AD).
statements.headline: Mithraism, a mystery cult of the god Mithras, was practised across the Roman Empire.

statements.subject: mithraism
statements.evidence: The Roman cult of Mithras is widely — though debatedly — linked to the older Indo-Iranian deity Mithra.
statements.headline: Roman Mithraism took its name and god from the ancient Indo-Iranian deity Mithra.

statements.subject: mithra
statements.evidence: Mithra is a yazata (divine being) venerated within Zoroastrianism.
statements.headline: The god Mithra is a divine being venerated within Zoroastrianism.

statements.subject: zoroastrianism
statements.evidence: Zoroastrianism arose in and was the state religion of ancient Persia.
statements.headline: Zoroastrianism arose in and became the state religion of ancient Persia.

statements.subject: persia
statements.evidence: Persia lay astride the Silk Road, linking the Mediterranean to China.
statements.headline: Persia lay astride the Silk Road, linking the Mediterranean to China.

statements.subject: great_wall_of_china
statements.evidence: The Great Wall protected merchants and garrisons along the northern Silk Road.
statements.headline: The Great Wall of China guarded merchants along the northern Silk Road.

statements.subject: roman_empire
statements.evidence: The Roman Empire succeeded the Roman Republic in 27 BC.
statements.headline: In 27 BC the Roman Republic gave way to the Roman Empire.

statements.subject: roman_empire
statements.evidence: Rome was the capital of the Roman Empire.
statements.headline: Rome was the capital of the Roman Empire.

statements.subject: julio_claudian_dynasty
statements.evidence: The Julio-Claudians were the first imperial dynasty of the Roman Empire.
statements.headline: The Julio-Claudians were the first imperial dynasty of the Roman Empire.

statements.subject: augustus
statements.evidence: Augustus founded the Julio-Claudian dynasty.
statements.headline: Augustus founded the Julio-Claudian dynasty, Rome's first imperial line.

statements.subject: augustus
statements.evidence: Augustus was the adopted heir of Julius Caesar.
statements.headline: Augustus rose to power as the adopted heir of Julius Caesar.

statements.subject: julius_caesar
statements.evidence: Julius Caesar was a statesman of the late Roman Republic.
statements.headline: Julius Caesar was a statesman of the late Roman Republic.

statements.subject: hadrian
statements.evidence: Hadrian was a Roman emperor (r. 117–138).
statements.headline: Hadrian ruled the Roman Empire at its height (r. 117-138).

statements.subject: hadrians_wall
statements.evidence: Hadrian's Wall is named after the emperor who ordered it.
statements.headline: Hadrian's Wall is named after the emperor who ordered it built.

statements.subject: hadrians_wall
statements.evidence: Hadrian's Wall marked the northern frontier of the Roman Empire in Britain.
statements.headline: Hadrian's Wall marked the Roman Empire's northern frontier in Britain.

statements.subject: hadrians_wall
statements.evidence: A speculative popular claim that Roman frontier-wall thinking echoed traveller accounts of China's Great Wall.
statements.headline: A speculative popular claim links Hadrian's Wall to traveller accounts of the Great Wall of China.

statements.subject: rome
statements.evidence: Roman legend held that the city was named after its founder Romulus.
statements.headline: Roman legend held the city of Rome was named after its founder Romulus.

statements.subject: romulus
statements.evidence: Roman foundation legend traced Romulus's lineage to the Trojan hero Aeneas.
statements.headline: Roman legend traced Romulus's line to the Trojan hero Aeneas.

statements.subject: aeneas
statements.evidence: Aeneas was a Trojan hero of the Trojan War.
statements.headline: Aeneas was a Trojan hero of the Trojan War who, in legend, carried Troy's line to Italy.

statements.subject: trojan_war
statements.evidence: The Trojan War was fought at Troy.
statements.headline: The Trojan War was fought at the city of Troy.

statements.subject: latin
statements.evidence: Latin originated in Latium, the region around Rome.
statements.headline: Latin originated in Latium, the region around Rome.

statements.subject: romance_languages
statements.evidence: The Romance languages descend from Vulgar Latin.
statements.headline: The Romance languages all descend from the Vulgar Latin of Rome.

statements.subject: latin
statements.evidence: Latin descends from Proto-Indo-European via the Italic branch.
statements.headline: Latin descends, via the Italic branch, from Proto-Indo-European.

statements.subject: mithra
statements.evidence: The deity Mithra descends from a Proto-Indo-Iranian god, itself rooted in Proto-Indo-European religion.
statements.headline: The god Mithra traces back to Proto-Indo-European religion.

statements.subject: mithra
statements.evidence: The god Mitra is invoked in the Rigveda.
statements.headline: The god Mitra is invoked in the Rigveda, India's oldest scripture.

statements.subject: great_wall_of_china
statements.evidence: Qin Shi Huang unified earlier border walls into a single Great Wall.
statements.headline: Qin Shi Huang joined earlier border walls into the first Great Wall of China.

statements.subject: qin_shi_huang
statements.evidence: Qin Shi Huang founded the Qin dynasty.
statements.headline: Qin Shi Huang founded the Qin dynasty and China's first empire.

statements.subject: han_dynasty
statements.evidence: The Han dynasty succeeded the Qin dynasty.
statements.headline: The Han dynasty succeeded the short-lived Qin dynasty.

statements.subject: silk_road
statements.evidence: Zhang Qian's Han-dynasty missions opened the Silk Road to the west.
statements.headline: Zhang Qian's Han-dynasty missions opened the Silk Road to the west.

statements.subject: zhang_qian
statements.evidence: Zhang Qian served the Han dynasty.
statements.headline: Zhang Qian served the Han dynasty as its westward envoy.

statements.subject: chang_an
statements.evidence: Chang'an was the eastern terminus of the Silk Road.
statements.headline: Chang'an was the eastern terminus of the Silk Road.

statements.subject: great_wall_of_china
statements.evidence: The Great Wall runs across northern China.
statements.headline: The Great Wall runs across northern China.

statements.subject: chang_an
statements.evidence: Chang'an (modern Xi'an) lies in China.
statements.headline: Chang'an (modern Xi'an) lies in China.

statements.subject: roman_empire
statements.evidence: Rome imported Chinese silk along the Silk Road, calling the distant land Serica.
statements.headline: The Roman Empire imported Chinese silk along the Silk Road, calling the distant land Serica.

statements.subject: amber_road
statements.evidence: The Amber Road carried Baltic amber to the Roman world.
statements.headline: The Amber Road carried Baltic amber to the Roman Empire.

statements.subject: byzantine_empire
statements.evidence: The Byzantine Empire was the eastern continuation of the Roman Empire.
statements.headline: The Byzantine Empire was the eastern continuation of the Roman Empire, lasting until 1453.

statements.subject: constantine_the_great
statements.evidence: Constantine the Great was a Roman emperor.
statements.headline: Constantine the Great was a Roman emperor.

statements.subject: byzantine_empire
statements.evidence: Constantinople was the capital of the Byzantine Empire.
statements.headline: Constantinople was the capital of the Byzantine Empire.

statements.subject: constantinople
statements.evidence: Constantinople was named after the emperor Constantine.
statements.headline: Constantinople was named after the emperor Constantine the Great.

statements.subject: christianity
statements.evidence: Christianity became the state religion of the Roman Empire in AD 380.
statements.headline: Christianity became the state religion of the Roman Empire in AD 380.

statements.subject: mithraism
statements.evidence: Scholars have long compared Mithraic and early Christian iconography and rites.
statements.headline: Scholars have long compared the iconography and rites of Mithraism and early Christianity.

statements.subject: jagannatha_samrat
statements.evidence: Jagannatha Samrat's Rekhaganita is a Sanskrit rendering of Euclid's Elements.
statements.headline: Jagannatha Samrat's Sanskrit Rekhaganita rendered Euclid's Elements for an Indian court.

statements.subject: jagannatha_samrat
statements.evidence: The Rekhaganita was based on al-Tusi's Arabic recension of the Elements, not the Greek directly.
statements.headline: Jagannatha's Sanskrit Euclid came via al-Tusi's Arabic recension, not the Greek text.

statements.subject: al_tusi
statements.evidence: Al-Tusi produced a widely-transmitted Arabic edition (tahrir) of Euclid's Elements.
statements.headline: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi's Arabic edition carried Euclid's Elements across the medieval world.

statements.subject: jagannatha_samrat
statements.evidence: Jagannatha served at the court of Jai Singh II, who commissioned the Sanskrit translation.
statements.headline: Jagannatha Samrat served Jai Singh II, who commissioned his Sanskrit Euclid.

statements.subject: al_tusi
statements.evidence: Al-Tusi worked at Maragheh in Persia, where he built his observatory.
statements.headline: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi built his great observatory at Maragheh in Persia.

statements.subject: euclid
statements.evidence: Euclid was active in Alexandria during the reign of Ptolemy I (c. 300 BC).
statements.headline: Euclid worked in Alexandria around 300 BC, where he wrote the Elements.

statements.subject: alexandria
statements.evidence: Alexandria was founded in 331 BC by, and named after, Alexander the Great.
statements.headline: Alexandria was founded in 331 BC by, and named after, Alexander the Great.

statements.subject: alexander_the_great
statements.evidence: Alexander the Great conquered the Achaemenid Persian Empire by 330 BC.
statements.headline: Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire by 330 BC.

statements.subject: alexander_the_great
statements.evidence: Alexander's campaign reached the Indus valley in north-western India in 326 BC.
statements.headline: Alexander the Great marched as far as the Indus in India in 326 BC.

statements.subject: buddhism
statements.evidence: Buddhism originated in ancient India in the 5th century BC.
statements.headline: Buddhism began in ancient India in the 5th century BC.

statements.subject: buddhism
statements.evidence: Buddhism spread from India through Central Asia into China along the Silk Road.
statements.headline: Buddhism spread from India to China along the Silk Road.

statements.subject: rigveda
statements.evidence: The Rigveda was composed in the north-western Indian subcontinent (c. 1500 BC).
statements.headline: The Rigveda was composed in north-western India around 1500 BC.

statements.subject: jai_singh_ii
statements.evidence: Jai Singh II ruled the kingdom of Amber (later Jaipur) in northern India.
statements.headline: Jai Singh II ruled Amber (later Jaipur) in northern India and built its observatories.

statements.subject: aristotle
statements.evidence: Aristotle studied for two decades under Plato at the Academy in Athens.
statements.headline: Aristotle studied for two decades under Plato at Athens.

statements.subject: alexander_the_great
statements.evidence: Aristotle was the personal tutor of the young Alexander the Great.
statements.headline: Aristotle was the personal tutor of the young Alexander the Great.

statements.subject: plato
statements.evidence: Plato founded his Academy on the outskirts of Athens.
statements.headline: Plato founded his Academy at Athens.

statements.subject: aristotle
statements.evidence: Aristotle taught at the Lyceum in Athens.
statements.headline: Aristotle taught at the Lyceum in Athens.

statements.subject: athens
statements.evidence: Athens was the pre-eminent city-state of Ancient Greece.
statements.headline: Athens was the pre-eminent city-state of Ancient Greece.

statements.subject: roman_empire
statements.evidence: Roman religion, art, and philosophy were profoundly shaped by Ancient Greece.
statements.headline: Rome's religion, art and philosophy were profoundly shaped by Ancient Greece.

statements.subject: greek_mythology
statements.evidence: Greek mythology is the body of myths belonging to Ancient Greece.
statements.headline: Greek mythology is the body of myths of Ancient Greece.

statements.subject: trojan_war
statements.evidence: The Trojan War is a central episode of Greek mythology.
statements.headline: The Trojan War is a central episode of Greek mythology.

statements.subject: trojan_war
statements.evidence: The Trojan War is recounted in Homer's Iliad and Odyssey.
statements.headline: The Trojan War is recounted in Homer's Iliad and Odyssey.

statements.subject: homer
statements.evidence: Homer was a poet of archaic Ancient Greece.
statements.headline: Homer was the poet of archaic Ancient Greece.

statements.subject: cleopatra
statements.evidence: Cleopatra ruled the Ptolemaic kingdom from its capital, Alexandria.
statements.headline: Cleopatra ruled Egypt from its capital, Alexandria.

statements.subject: cleopatra
statements.evidence: Cleopatra VII was the last active pharaoh of Ancient Egypt.
statements.headline: Cleopatra was the last active pharaoh of Ancient Egypt.

statements.subject: ancient_egypt
statements.evidence: Egypt was annexed as the Roman province of Aegyptus in 30 BC after Cleopatra's defeat.
statements.headline: Ancient Egypt became a Roman province in 30 BC after Cleopatra's defeat.

statements.subject: alexandria
statements.evidence: Alexandria was the capital of Ptolemaic and Roman Egypt.
statements.headline: Alexandria was the capital of Ptolemaic and Roman Egypt.

statements.subject: nile
statements.evidence: The Nile and its annual flood were the lifeblood of Ancient Egypt.
statements.headline: The Nile's annual flood was the lifeblood of Ancient Egypt.

statements.subject: great_pyramid_of_giza
statements.evidence: The Great Pyramid was raised at Giza during Egypt's Old Kingdom.
statements.headline: The Great Pyramid was raised at Giza in Ancient Egypt's Old Kingdom.

statements.subject: egyptian_hieroglyphs
statements.evidence: Hieroglyphs were the formal writing system of Ancient Egypt.
statements.headline: Hieroglyphs were the formal writing system of Ancient Egypt.

statements.subject: al_khwarizmi
statements.evidence: Al-Khwarizmi was a scholar at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad.
statements.headline: Al-Khwarizmi was a scholar at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad.

statements.subject: house_of_wisdom
statements.evidence: The House of Wisdom was the great library and academy of Abbasid Baghdad.
statements.headline: The House of Wisdom was the great library and academy of Abbasid Baghdad.

statements.subject: house_of_wisdom
statements.evidence: The House of Wisdom was an institution of the Abbasid Caliphate.
statements.headline: The House of Wisdom was a scholarly institution of the Abbasid Caliphate.

statements.subject: baghdad
statements.evidence: Baghdad was founded in 762 as the capital of the Abbasid Caliphate.
statements.headline: Baghdad was founded in 762 as the capital of the Abbasid Caliphate.

statements.subject: house_of_wisdom
statements.evidence: The House of Wisdom translated and built upon Greek scholarship, including Euclid's Elements.
statements.headline: Baghdad's House of Wisdom translated and built upon the learning of Ancient Greece, including Euclid.

statements.subject: algebra
statements.evidence: The discipline of algebra — and the word itself, from al-jabr — derives from al-Khwarizmi's 9th-c. treatise.
statements.headline: Algebra - and the word itself, from al-jabr - comes from al-Khwarizmi's 9th-century treatise.

statements.subject: al_tusi
statements.evidence: Al-Tusi worked in the tradition of Islamic mathematics that al-Khwarizmi helped found.
statements.headline: Al-Tusi worked in the tradition of Islamic mathematics that al-Khwarizmi founded.

statements.subject: baghdad
statements.evidence: Baghdad was a major hub on the Silk Road trade network.
statements.headline: Baghdad was a major hub on the Silk Road.

statements.subject: ptolemy
statements.evidence: Ptolemy lived and worked in Roman Alexandria.
statements.headline: Ptolemy lived and worked in Roman Alexandria.

statements.subject: copernicus
statements.evidence: Copernicus built on, and ultimately overturned, Ptolemy's geocentric Almagest.
statements.headline: Copernicus built on, then overturned, Ptolemy's Earth-centred astronomy.

statements.subject: copernicus
statements.evidence: Copernicus's planetary models use the 'Tusi couple', the geometric device of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi.
statements.headline: Copernicus's planetary models use the 'Tusi couple', a device of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi.

statements.subject: galileo
statements.evidence: Galileo's telescopic observations championed Copernican heliocentrism.
statements.headline: Galileo's telescope championed Copernicus's Sun-centred cosmos.

statements.subject: isaac_newton
statements.evidence: Newton's mechanics built directly on Galileo's law of falling bodies.
statements.headline: Newton's mechanics built directly on Galileo's law of falling bodies.

statements.subject: isaac_newton
statements.evidence: Newton cast the Principia in the axiomatic geometric style of Euclid's Elements.
statements.headline: Newton cast the Principia in the axiomatic geometric style of Euclid's Elements.

statements.subject: confucius
statements.evidence: Confucius lived in the state of Lu in what is now eastern China (551–479 BC).
statements.headline: Confucius taught in the state of Lu in eastern China (551-479 BC).

statements.subject: confucianism
statements.evidence: Confucianism developed from the teachings of Confucius and his followers.
statements.headline: Confucianism grew from the teachings of Confucius.

statements.subject: tang_dynasty
statements.evidence: The Tang dynasty (618–907) ruled a unified China from its capital at Chang'an.
statements.headline: The Tang dynasty ruled a golden-age China from Chang'an (618-907).

statements.subject: tang_dynasty
statements.evidence: The Tang dynasty presided over a golden age of Silk Road trade between China and the west.
statements.headline: The Tang dynasty presided over a golden age of Silk Road trade.

statements.subject: tang_dynasty
statements.evidence: Tang governance and its imperial examination system were grounded in Confucian thought.
statements.headline: The Tang state and its imperial examinations were grounded in Confucianism.

statements.subject: japan
statements.evidence: Nara- and Heian-period Japan modelled its writing system, capital, and bureaucracy on Tang China.
statements.headline: Early Japan modelled its writing, capital and bureaucracy on Tang China.

statements.subject: zen
statements.evidence: Zen is a school of Mahayana Buddhism, descended from Chinese Chan.
statements.headline: Zen is a school of Mahayana Buddhism, descended from Chinese Chan.

statements.subject: zen
statements.evidence: Zen, descended from Chinese Chan, became the pre-eminent Buddhist tradition of medieval and modern Japan.
statements.headline: Zen, by way of Chinese Chan, became the pre-eminent Buddhism of medieval Japan.

statements.subject: odin
statements.evidence: Odin is the chief god of Norse mythology.
statements.headline: Odin is the chief god of Norse mythology.

statements.subject: thor
statements.evidence: Thor is the thunder-god of Norse mythology.
statements.headline: Thor is the thunder-god of Norse mythology.

statements.subject: loki
statements.evidence: Loki is the trickster god of Norse mythology.
statements.headline: Loki is the trickster god of Norse mythology.

statements.subject: thor
statements.evidence: In Norse mythology Thor is the son of Odin.
statements.headline: In Norse myth, the thunder-god Thor is the son of Odin.

statements.subject: norse_mythology
statements.evidence: Comparative Indo-European studies trace Norse mythology to a common Proto-Indo-European mythology.
statements.headline: Norse mythology traces back to a common Proto-Indo-European mythology.

statements.subject: celtic_mythology
statements.evidence: Celtic mythology shares the common Proto-Indo-European mythological heritage.
statements.headline: Celtic mythology shares the common Proto-Indo-European mythological heritage.

statements.subject: thor
statements.evidence: Thor, the Norse thunder-god, is cognate with the Vedic thunder-god Indra of the Rigveda, both from a Proto-Indo-European storm deity.
statements.headline: The Norse thunder-god Thor is cognate with the Rigveda's Indra, both from a Proto-Indo-European storm god.

statements.subject: cai_lun
statements.evidence: Cai Lun was a court official of the Han dynasty.
statements.headline: Cai Lun, credited with paper, was a court official of the Han dynasty.

statements.subject: paper
statements.evidence: Papermaking was traditionally credited to Cai Lun, who standardized the process c. 105 AD.
statements.headline: Paper was standardised by the Han official Cai Lun around 105 AD.

statements.subject: paper
statements.evidence: Papermaking spread westward from China to the Islamic world along the Silk Road.
statements.headline: Papermaking spread west from China to the Islamic world along the Silk Road.

statements.subject: woodblock_printing
statements.evidence: Woodblock printing was developed in Tang-dynasty China.
statements.headline: Woodblock printing was developed in Tang-dynasty China.

statements.subject: woodblock_printing
statements.evidence: The spread of woodblock printing was driven by Buddhist demand for scriptures; the earliest dated printed book is the Buddhist Diamond Sutra (868).
statements.headline: Buddhist demand for scripture drove woodblock printing - the earliest dated printed book is the Diamond Sutra (868).

statements.subject: gunpowder
statements.evidence: Gunpowder was discovered by alchemists of Tang-dynasty China.
statements.headline: Gunpowder was discovered by alchemists of Tang-dynasty China.

statements.subject: compass
statements.evidence: The earliest Chinese compass, a lodestone 'south-pointer', is described in Han-dynasty texts.
statements.headline: The first compass, a Han-dynasty lodestone 'south-pointer', is described in Chinese texts.

statements.subject: mansa_musa
statements.evidence: Mansa Musa was the tenth mansa (emperor) of the Mali Empire.
statements.headline: Mansa Musa was the tenth emperor of the Mali Empire.

statements.subject: timbuktu
statements.evidence: Timbuktu was a major city of the Mali Empire under Mansa Musa.
statements.headline: Timbuktu was a great city of the Mali Empire.

statements.subject: timbuktu
statements.evidence: Mansa Musa annexed Timbuktu and commissioned its Djinguereber Mosque and scholarly institutions.
statements.headline: Mansa Musa annexed Timbuktu and endowed its mosque and scholars.

statements.subject: mali_empire
statements.evidence: The Mali Empire's wealth came from the trans-Saharan gold and salt trade.
statements.headline: The Mali Empire's wealth flowed from the trans-Saharan gold and salt trade.

statements.subject: timbuktu
statements.evidence: Timbuktu was a principal southern terminus of the trans-Saharan caravan trade.
statements.headline: Timbuktu was a southern terminus of the trans-Saharan caravan trade.

statements.subject: mansa_musa
statements.evidence: Mansa Musa was a devout Muslim whose 1324 hajj to Mecca made Mali renowned across the Islamic world.
statements.headline: Mansa Musa's 1324 hajj made the Muslim emperor of Mali famous across the Islamic world.

statements.subject: timbuktu
statements.evidence: Timbuktu became a celebrated centre of Islamic scholarship, home to the Sankore Madrasah.
statements.headline: Timbuktu became a celebrated centre of Islamic scholarship, home to the Sankore madrasa.

statements.subject: islam
statements.evidence: In Persia, Islam succeeded Zoroastrianism as the dominant religion after the 7th-century Muslim conquest.
statements.headline: In Persia, Islam displaced Zoroastrianism after the 7th-century Muslim conquest.

statements.subject: abbasid_caliphate
statements.evidence: The Abbasid Caliphate was the leading state of the medieval Sunni Muslim world.
statements.headline: The Abbasid Caliphate led the medieval Sunni Muslim world.

statements.subject: elizabeth_ii
statements.evidence: Elizabeth II descended from Alfred the Great through the recorded line of English monarchs.
statements.headline: Elizabeth II descends from Alfred the Great through the recorded line of English monarchs.

statements.subject: alfred_the_great
statements.evidence: Alfred the Great was a king of the House of Wessex.
statements.headline: Alfred the Great was a king of the House of Wessex.

statements.subject: house_of_wessex
statements.evidence: The Anglo-Saxon royal genealogies traced the House of Wessex's line back to the god Woden — the Old English form of Odin.
statements.headline: Anglo-Saxon genealogies traced the House of Wessex back to the god Woden - Odin.

statements.subject: naruhito
statements.evidence: The Japanese imperial line, of which Naruhito is the 126th monarch, is traditionally traced to the legendary first emperor Jimmu.
statements.headline: Emperor Naruhito's line is traditionally traced to Japan's legendary first emperor, Jimmu.

statements.subject: jimmu
statements.evidence: Jimmu is held in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki to be a descendant of the sun goddess Amaterasu.
statements.headline: Japan's legendary first emperor Jimmu is held to descend from the sun goddess Amaterasu.

statements.subject: amaterasu
statements.evidence: Amaterasu is the sun goddess of Shinto, enshrined at Ise.
statements.headline: Amaterasu is the sun goddess of Shinto, enshrined at Ise.

statements.subject: shinto
statements.evidence: Shinto is the indigenous religion of Japan.
statements.headline: Shinto is the indigenous religion of Japan.

statements.subject: renaissance
statements.evidence: The Renaissance began in 14th-century Florence before spreading across Europe.
statements.headline: The Renaissance began in 14th-century Florence.

statements.subject: renaissance_humanism
statements.evidence: Humanism — the revival of classical letters — was the defining intellectual programme of the Renaissance.
statements.headline: Humanism, the revival of classical letters, was the Renaissance's defining programme.

statements.subject: renaissance_humanism
statements.evidence: Marsilio Ficino's Florentine Platonic Academy, funded by the Medici, translated Plato's complete works into Latin; Platonism became central to Renaissance humanist thought.
statements.headline: The Medici-funded Florentine Academy translated Plato, making Platonism central to Renaissance humanism.

statements.subject: renaissance_humanism
statements.evidence: Humanists took the literature and philosophy of classical Greece as their model for study and imitation.
statements.headline: Renaissance humanists took the literature and philosophy of Ancient Greece as their model.

statements.subject: renaissance
statements.evidence: Byzantine scholars carried Greek manuscripts to Italy around the fall of 1453; the exodus (already under way beforehand) reinforced the Western revival of Greek learning.
statements.headline: Greek scholars fleeing the 1453 fall of Constantinople carried classical manuscripts into Renaissance Italy.

statements.subject: fall_of_constantinople
statements.evidence: The 1453 siege and capture took place at Constantinople itself.
statements.headline: The 1453 siege that ended Byzantium took place at Constantinople itself.

statements.subject: fall_of_constantinople
statements.evidence: The Ottoman capture of the city in 1453 ended the Byzantine Empire.
statements.headline: The Ottoman capture of the city in 1453 ended the Byzantine Empire.

statements.subject: leonardo_da_vinci
statements.evidence: Leonardo is the archetypal figure of the High Renaissance.
statements.headline: Leonardo da Vinci is the archetypal figure of the High Renaissance.

statements.subject: leonardo_da_vinci
statements.evidence: Leonardo trained in Florence in Verrocchio's workshop and worked there for much of his career.
statements.headline: Leonardo da Vinci trained and worked in Renaissance Florence.

statements.subject: michelangelo
statements.evidence: Michelangelo is, with Leonardo and Raphael, one of the defining artists of the High Renaissance.
statements.headline: Michelangelo was one of the defining artists of the High Renaissance.

statements.subject: michelangelo
statements.evidence: Michelangelo was raised in Florence and carved the David there under Medici patronage.
statements.headline: Michelangelo carved his David in Florence under Medici patronage.

statements.subject: mona_lisa
statements.evidence: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci, begun in Florence c. 1503.
statements.headline: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci, begun in Florence c. 1503.

statements.subject: house_of_medici
statements.evidence: The Medici were the banking dynasty of Florence and its effective rulers from 1434.
statements.headline: The Medici were the banking dynasty that ruled Renaissance Florence.

statements.subject: renaissance
statements.evidence: Medici patronage funded the artists, architects and Platonist scholars of the Florentine Renaissance.
statements.headline: Medici patronage funded the artists and scholars of the Florentine Renaissance.

statements.subject: printing_press
statements.evidence: Johannes Gutenberg introduced the movable-type printing press at Mainz c. 1440.
statements.headline: Johannes Gutenberg built the movable-type printing press at Mainz around 1440.

statements.subject: printing_press
statements.evidence: Gutenberg's press printed on paper — a Chinese invention that reached Europe through the Islamic world, where Abbasid papermaking followed the battle of Talas (751).
statements.headline: Gutenberg's printing press ran on paper - a Chinese invention that reached Europe through the Islamic world.

statements.subject: renaissance
statements.evidence: The press made humanist and classical texts cheap and uniform, spreading Renaissance learning across Europe.
statements.headline: The printing press spread Renaissance learning by making classical texts cheap and uniform.

statements.subject: copernicus
statements.evidence: Copernicus (1473-1543) studied at Bologna and Padua and worked within Renaissance humanist learning.
statements.headline: Copernicus studied in Renaissance Italy, within its humanist learning.

statements.subject: hinduism
statements.evidence: Hinduism arose on the Indian subcontinent, its earliest layer in the Vedic period.
statements.headline: Hinduism arose on the Indian subcontinent in the Vedic age.

statements.subject: hinduism
statements.evidence: The Rigveda, oldest of the Vedas, is the foundational scripture from which later Hindu tradition grew.
statements.headline: The Rigveda is the foundational scripture from which Hinduism grew.

statements.subject: sanskrit
statements.evidence: Sanskrit is an Indo-Aryan language descended from Proto-Indo-European.
statements.headline: Sanskrit is an Indo-Aryan tongue descended from Proto-Indo-European.

statements.subject: sanskrit
statements.evidence: Sanskrit developed in the Indian subcontinent as the classical language of Hindu, Buddhist and Jain scripture.
statements.headline: Sanskrit developed in India as the classical language of Hindu, Buddhist and Jain scripture.

statements.subject: maurya_empire
statements.evidence: The Maurya Empire unified most of the Indian subcontinent from its capital at Pataliputra.
statements.headline: The Maurya Empire was the first to unify most of the Indian subcontinent.

statements.subject: maurya_empire
statements.evidence: Chandragupta founded the Maurya Empire in the wake of Alexander's withdrawal from the Indus valley in 323 BC.
statements.headline: Chandragupta founded the Maurya Empire in the wake of Alexander's retreat from India.

statements.subject: ashoka
statements.evidence: Ashoka was the third emperor of the Maurya Empire, ruling most of the Indian subcontinent.
statements.headline: Ashoka was the great third emperor of the Maurya Empire.

statements.subject: ashoka
statements.evidence: After the Kalinga War, Ashoka embraced Buddhism and propagated it across and beyond his empire.
statements.headline: After the Kalinga War, Ashoka embraced Buddhism and spread it across Asia.

statements.subject: chola_dynasty
statements.evidence: The Chola dynasty ruled southern India from the Coromandel coast.
statements.headline: The Chola dynasty ruled southern India from the Coromandel coast.

statements.subject: chola_dynasty
statements.evidence: Chola naval power dominated the Bay of Bengal, controlling the maritime trade that linked India to the sea routes of the Silk Road.
statements.headline: Chola sea power dominated the Bay of Bengal on the maritime Silk Road.

statements.subject: srivijaya
statements.evidence: Srivijaya commanded the Strait of Malacca, the maritime Silk Road chokepoint between India and China.
statements.headline: Srivijaya commanded the Strait of Malacca, the maritime Silk Road chokepoint.

statements.subject: srivijaya
statements.evidence: Srivijaya was a major centre of Mahayana Buddhist learning that drew pilgrims such as Yijing.
statements.headline: Srivijaya was a great centre of Mahayana Buddhist learning.

statements.subject: khmer_empire
statements.evidence: The Khmer Empire's kingship and state temples were rooted in Hindu cosmology.
statements.headline: The Khmer Empire's kingship and temples were rooted in Hindu cosmology.

statements.subject: angkor_wat
statements.evidence: Angkor Wat was built in the early 12th century as the state temple of the Khmer Empire.
statements.headline: Angkor Wat was built as the state temple of the Khmer Empire.

statements.subject: angkor_wat
statements.evidence: Angkor Wat was originally consecrated to the Hindu god Vishnu.
statements.headline: Angkor Wat was first consecrated to the Hindu god Vishnu.

statements.subject: borobudur
statements.evidence: Borobudur is a 9th-century Mahayana Buddhist monument in central Java.
statements.headline: Borobudur is a 9th-century Mahayana Buddhist monument in Java.

statements.subject: borobudur
statements.evidence: Buddhism reached Java along the maritime trade routes feeding the Silk Road, the milieu that raised Borobudur.
statements.headline: Borobudur rose in the milieu of the maritime trade routes feeding the Silk Road.
